parts.title: Transcript
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Speaker 1: You now have some time to look at the questions on your screen.

Speaker 2: Now answer the questions on your screen.

Speaker 3: The story began a few days before Christmas in 1938, when the first living coelacanth was discovered off the east coast of South Africa at the mouth of the Chalumna River.

Speaker 1: The fish was caught in a shark gill net by Captain Goosen and his crew, who, recognising the bizarre nature of their catch, alerted the local museum in the small South African town of East London.

Speaker 2: The director of the East London Museum at the time was Miss Marjorie Courtney-Latimer, after whom the coelacanth was eventually named.

Speaker 3: Miss Courtney-Latimer offered bounties to fishermen for unfamiliar fish.

Speaker 1: It was Miss Courtney-Latimer who alerted the prominent South African ichthyologist Dr JLB Smith, who initially identified the fish and subsequently informed the world about this amazing discovery.

Speaker 2: This first coelacanth led to the discovery of the first documented population off the remote Comoros Islands between the mainland of Africa and Madagascar.

Speaker 3: For 60 years this was presumed to be the only coelacanth population in existence.

Speaker 1: Originally it was a concern that the coelacanth might have a very limited range and that overfishing along the Comoros Islands might wipe it out.

Speaker 2: However, scientists were amazed when, on July the 30th, 1998, an American scientist discovered a coelacanth population in Indonesia.

Speaker 3: Dr Mark Erdman was on a honeymoon trip to the area investigating a coral reef research site when he spotted a strange fish being wheeled into the fish market.

Speaker 1: He recognised the fish as a coelacanth and snapped a picture before it was sold.
